expectations.text: Identifies that hold_soc_state and its expiry timer are in-memory only and lost on restart/reload
expectations.evidence: "tracks the hold only in in-memory state (hold_soc_state dict + an asyncio expiry timer)"; "timer and state die with the process"

expectations.text: Identifies that persist_force_mode_state serializes only force charge/discharge (Hold writes None)
expectations.evidence: "persist_force_mode_state() ... serializes only force charge/discharge — with only Hold active it writes force_mode_state = None, affirmatively recording 'nothing active' (and clobbering any prior persisted force state)"

expectations.text: Identifies that no startup path restores or exits a stale hold
expectations.evidence: "restore_force_mode_from_persistence (~L23640) gets None and early-returns"; "_restore_disabled_optimizer_reserve_if_stale (L117) hard-excludes sigenergy at L129 and is reserve-keyed — STANDBY never touches the reserve, so it's structurally invisible"
"with the optimizer disabled there is no 5-minute cycle to overwrite it"; "Optimizer-enabled installs self-repair in ~5 min"

expectations.text: Fix outline includes persisting hold state with expiry and restoring/exiting on setup
expectations.evidence: "Persist hold_soc_state in persist_force_mode_state (mode 'hold_soc', absolute UTC expiry...)"; "Restore-or-exit in restore_force_mode_from_persistence: future expiry → re-arm timer... expired → brand restore with the _restore_pre_idle_backup_reserve retry contract" plus a runtime guard for existing installs

notes: 5/5. Extras: per-brand blast radius table, OB-7 adjacency, playbook compensating-mechanism sweep, immediate user remediation, confirming log-line evidence. Cross-checked registry (OB-5) then verified at HEAD rather than trusting it.
